original_hypothesis.title: lockOnBehalf Can Overwrite Recipient's Lock Duration Preference
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In _lock, if the lock recipient's playerSettings.lockDuration is 0 (never set by the user), _lockDuration falls back to lockdrop.minLockDuration. At the end of _lock, playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration is unconditionally set to this value. An attacker can use lockOnBehalf to force-set a victim's lock duration to the lockdrop minimum before the victim sets their own preference.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: The combination of the fallback logic and the unconditional write to playerSettings means any caller of lockOnBehalf can influence the recipient's future lock behavior. This is a sovereignty violation where the recipient's preferences are overwritten without consent.
preconditions: Recipient is a registered main account

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: Unconditional write to recipient's playerSettings from lockOnBehalf
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: Attacker sets victim's lockDuration to lockdrop.minLockDuration before victim configures their own preference
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: Should only write playerSettings for msg.sender, not for _lockRecipient in lockOnBehalf

analysis_questions: Can the victim override this by calling setLockDuration afterward?
Does this interact with the unlockTime griefing (TECH-H002)?
What is the practical impact on victim's future locks?
validation_requirements: Call lockOnBehalf for a new user who hasn't set lockDuration
Verify victim's playerSettings.lockDuration is now set to lockdrop.minLockDuration
Verify victim must explicitly call setLockDuration to change it

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: New user registers. Attacker calls lockOnBehalf(token, 0, victim) before victim sets lockDuration. Victim's lockDuration is set to lockdrop.minLockDuration (e.g., 30 days). Victim later locks large amount expecting to use a longer duration but doesn't realize their duration was already set.

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Victim may lock with unintended short duration, affecting reward calculations
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: New users who haven't yet configured lock duration
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Victim can call setLockDuration to fix

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No test for lockOnBehalf's effect on recipient's playerSettings

validation_result.impact_summary: Any caller can force-set a victim's playerSettings.lockDuration to lockdrop.minLockDuration via lockOnBehalf before the victim configures their own preference, violating the recipient's settings sovereignty.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The _lock function reads the recipient's playerSettings.lockDuration, falls back to lockdrop.minLockDuration if it's 0, and then unconditionally writes this derived value back to playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration at line 349. Since lockOnBehalf allows any caller to specify an arbitrary _onBehalfOf address as the lock recipient, an attacker can invoke this function to write lockdrop.minLockDuration into a victim's playerSettings before the victim has configured their own duration. This is a real code defect: the function modifies another user's persistent settings without their consent. The victim can later call setLockDuration to fix this, but the initial overwrite is unauthorized and could lead to unintended lock durations on subsequent locks if the victim doesn't realize their settings were changed.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Lines 319-323 show the fallback logic: `uint32 _lockDuration = playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration; if (_lockDuration == 0) { _lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration; }`. Line 349 shows the unconditional write: `playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration = _lockDuration;`. The lockOnBehalf function at lines 275-291 sets lockRecipient to _onBehalfOf with no authorization check on modifying that recipient's settings. Together, these constitute a confirmed defect where an external caller can modify another user's persistent configuration.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: When the recipient's lockDuration is 0 (unset), it falls back to lockdrop.minLockDuration and then unconditionally writes this value to the recipient's playerSettings. Since lockOnBehalf allows any caller to trigger _lock for any recipient, this enables unauthorized modification of the victim's settings.

No check prevents arbitrary callers from modifying the recipient's playerSettings through the _lock function. Any address can be specified as _onBehalfOf, and the subsequent _lock call will write to that address's playerSettings.

execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: lockOnBehalf → _lock: At line 319, `uint32 _lockDuration = playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration;` reads 0 for the victim. Lines 321-323 fall back: `if (_lockDuration == 0) { _lockDuration = lockdrop.minLockDuration; }`. Line 349 writes: `playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration = _lockDuration;`. This overwrites the victim's unset preference with the lockdrop minimum duration without their consent.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: The start-lockdrop.ts script configures a 30-day lockdrop. The configure-token-test.ts script configures tokens with active=true. These confirm that lockdrop.minLockDuration would be non-zero during active lockdrops, making the fallback meaningful.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: Attack scenario: 1) A lockdrop is active with minLockDuration = 30 days. 2) Victim registers as a player but has not yet called setLockDuration (lockDuration = 0). 3) Attacker calls lockOnBehalf(tokenAddress, 1 wei equivalent, victimAddress) sending minimal tokens. 4) _lock reads victim's lockDuration as 0, falls back to 30 days. 5) playerSettings[victim].lockDuration is now set to 30 days. 6) Victim later locks large amount expecting to use a different duration, but their settings already show 30 days. If victim doesn't check or realize, they lock at an unintended duration. This is a valid attack that costs only the gas fee plus a minimal token amount.
validation_result.impact_assessment: The direct impact is that an attacker can set a victim's lock duration to lockdrop.minLockDuration before the victim sets their own preference. This affects subsequent lock operations for the victim. The victim may unknowingly lock with a shorter duration than intended, potentially receiving fewer rewards. While the victim can call setLockDuration to correct this, the unauthorized modification of settings is a real code defect. The severity is medium because recovery is possible and the impact is limited to settings manipulation rather than direct fund loss.
recommended_mitigations: Only write to playerSettings[_lockRecipient].lockDuration when msg.sender == _lockRecipient (i.e., in the lock() path, not lockOnBehalf)
In _lock, separate the lockDuration resolution from the settings write: use _lockDuration for the current lock computation but do not persist it to playerSettings when the caller is not the recipient
Add a require(_quantity > 0) check to prevent zero-amount locks that only serve to modify settings
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy LockManager with an active lockdrop (minLockDuration = 30 days). 2. Register victim via AccountManager. 3. Verify victim's playerSettings.lockDuration == 0 via getPlayerSettings. 4. From attacker EOA, call lockOnBehalf(configuredToken, 1, victimAddress) with appropriate approval/value. 5. Check victim's playerSettings.lockDuration via getPlayerSettings — should now be 30 days. 6. Confirm victim did not call setLockDuration.
uncertainties: The exact value of lockdrop.minLockDuration in production is not specified in deployment scripts (start-lockdrop.ts calls startLockdrop action which is not provided)
If lockdrop.minLockDuration is 0 (no active lockdrop), the overwrite writes 0 which is the default — no impact in that case
additional_testing_needed: Test that lockOnBehalf with zero quantity still triggers the settings overwrite for ERC20 tokens
Verify whether the settings overwrite persists across multiple lockOnBehalf calls from different attackers
Test interaction with setLockDuration — can victim override the attacker-set value?
